content:
First rewrite the divisibility condition

 abc ∣ (ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1).

Set S=ab+bc+ca+1.  One checks easily

 (ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1) = a^2b^2c^2 + abc(a+b+c) + S,

so

 (ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1)/(abc) = abc + (a+b+c) + S/(abc).

Hence we need S=ab+bc+ca+1 to be a multiple of abc.  Write

 ab+bc+ca+1 = k·abc,    k∈ℕ.

Notice for k≥2, one finds 1/a+1/b+1/c+1/(abc)≥2 ⇒ at least one of a,b,c=1.  A small‐case check shows the only solutions are, up to permutation,

  k=1: {2,3,7},

  k=2: {1,2,3},

  k=3: {1,1,2},

  k=4: {1,1,1}.

Counting ordered triples gives

  {2,3,7} ⇒ 3! = 6  
  {1,2,3} ⇒ 3! = 6  
  {1,1,2} ⇒ 3 ways  
  {1,1,1} ⇒ 1 way  

Total = 6+6+3+1 = 16.  

Answer: \boxed{16}.
